title: Inverse generalised spin models of answers to questionnaires
authors: Arianna Armanetti, Luca Cecchetti, Paolo Sarti, Diego Garlaschelli, Miguel Ibáñez-Berganza
abstract: Network psychometrics conceptualises psychological constructs as emergent properties of systems of interacting variables. Energy-based probabilistic models have gained popularity as models of these interactions, but their psychometric application has so far been limited, since most implementations assume binary or ternary responses and rely on limiting inference assumptions. We infer and analyse three generalised spin models of ordinal questionnaire data: the generalised Ising, Blume-Capel (BC), and Blume-Emery-Griffiths (BEG) models. We prove the concavity of the maximum likelihood estimation of the parameters, as well as the gauge invariance of the Ising and BC models. Afterwards, we propose an inference protocol of approximated likelihood maximisation, based on the Monte Carlo estimation of the likelihood gradients. We apply this procedure to eleven psychometric and sociological questionnaires, comparing the inferred spin models against the multivariate Gaussian. We then assess whether the inferred models reproduce the empirical features of the data in terms of principal-component histograms, and histograms of Euclidean and Mahalanobis distances to the mean answer. The multi-modality observed in the histograms of principal components is partially captured by the spin models. This trait of polarisation can be understood, in the light of mean-field theory, as coexistence of stable and metastable phases of the spin models. The BEG model systematically outperforms the other models in capturing the distribution of distances to the mean, while all models underestimate the heavy tails of the Mahalanobis distance. Overall, the analysis witnesses the predictive power of the BEG model, able to account better than others for the abundance of outliers and mean responders, and reveals highly non-linear features of questionnaire data that both Gaussian and spin models fail to account for.
summary: This work applies advanced statistical physics tools, specifically generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG), to analyze ordinal data from questionnaires. By treating psychological constructs as emergent properties of interacting variables, the authors show that the BEG model best captures complex structural features like multi-modality and distance distributions, surpassing simpler Gaussian assumptions.
main_problem: The paper addresses how to model complex, ordinal data from psychological questionnaires by treating psychological constructs as emergent properties of interacting variables.
main_result: The Blume-Emery-Griffiths (BEG) model systematically outperforms simpler models (like Ising or Gaussian) in capturing the distribution of distances to the mean, suggesting its predictive power for complex data structures.
method: The authors employ maximum entropy principles, mapping the problem onto generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) and using advanced inference protocols like Monte Carlo estimation of likelihood gradients.
model_or_system: The core system is the generalized spin model, defined by Hamiltonians incorporating linear, quadratic, and biquadratic couplings (BEG). These models are applied to ordinal questionnaire data, allowing for interactions beyond simple pairwise correlations.
key_observables: Principal-component histograms, histograms of Euclidean and Mahalanobis distances to the mean, and the model's ability to reproduce multi-modality.
important_parameters: The coupling constants ($\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{J}, \mathbf{K}$) defining the Hamiltonian, and the number of response options ($R$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumptions involve using maximum entropy models to constrain the system, and the necessity of using extended BEG models to maintain gauge invariance.
figures_summary: Figures compare item histograms and principal component histograms across various questionnaires, demonstrating the BEG model's superior fit to distance distributions compared to Gaussian or copula models.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the limitations of traditional psychometrics, introducing generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) as energy-based alternatives, detailing the inference protocols, and finally comparing the models' ability to reproduce empirical structural features like multi-modality and distance distributions.
why_interesting: While the application is in psychometrics, the mathematical framework—using mean-field theory and spin models to describe emergent collective behavior from local interactions—is directly analogous to modeling phase transitions or complex order parameters in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Histograms of the Euclidean distance to the mean, hdx, for the dass (left) and sd3 (right) questionnaires: comparison between empirical data (error-bars) and the three spin models (Ising, BC, BEG, u-step lines) learned with full-likelihood maximisation and sampled with MCMC. The BEG model accounts better than Ising and BC for the high probability density of subjects close to the mean (small dx) and far from the mean (large dx). Error bars are Wilson score confidence intervals at p = 0.05.

FIG. 2. Histogram of the first 6 principal components hx′ i (i ∈{1, . . . , 6}) for the rwas questionnaire: comparison between empirical data and the three spin models (Ising, BC, BEG). The Ising and BC models each exhibit two local maxima of the histogram hx′ 1 of the first principal component, the BEG model exhibits three local maxima, and the empirical histogram does not show multi-modal structure.

FIG. 3. Histogram of the Mahalanobis distance to the mean, hd(M) x , for the sd3 questionnaire: direct comparison between the BEG model, the copula model and the empirical distribution. Both models underestimate the frequency of subjects close to and far from the mean.

FIG. 4. The gcbs questionnaire exhibits multi-modal histograms of x′ 1 and of dx, captured by the BEG model. Left: histograms of the first factor hf1; the BEG model reproduces the tri-modal empirical structure, while the Ising and BC models do not (for figure clarity, the BC model is not shown, see Fig. 17 in Appendix J). Right: histogram of the Euclidean distance to the mean, hdx; the BEG model reproduces the second and third peaks visible in the empirical data, which are absent in the Ising and BC models.

FIG. 5. Histogram of the Mahalanobis distance to the mean, hd(M) x (linear scale), for the rwas questionnaire. Left: comparison between the three spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) and the empirical distribution; only the BEG model accounts for the empirical peak of subjects near the origin for small d(M) x . Right: same comparison for the simple models (gauss, gauss-disc, copulas, cat-ind); the copulas and gauss-disc also reproduce the near-origin peak, while the gauss and cat-ind models predict a maximum at a much higher Mahalanobis distance.
